---
title: |
  | Lecture 17
  | Choosing a test
subtitle: "ABD 3e Interleaf 7 (p. 368)"
description: "Summarizes the decision process for choosing an appropriate statistical test based on the research question, data types, and study design. Provides a flowchart to guide test selection."
format:
  revealjs:
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 6
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

## Learning Objectives

## Which test should I use?

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   Very common situation: you have a question, you collected data, but
    now you don’t know what test to use to answer the question.

-   The following tables lay out your options.

-   In the future, you should be able to apply this thinking to your own
    research.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
-   For now, you should be able to read an example of a question and
    data set and decide which test is appropriate.

-   For examples of questions, see the Practice and Assignment problems
    at the end of each chapter.

-   Now imagine you read a question but didn’t know which chapter it
    came from. Could you choose the appropriate statistical procedure to
    answer the question?
:::
:::::

## Statistical tests for data on a [single categorial variable]{.keyword}

+--------------------------------+-----------------------------------+
| Goal                           | Test                              |
+================================+===================================+
| Use frequency data to test     | Binomial test ([Lecture           |
| whether a population           | 9](../lecture%                    |
| proportion equals a null       | 20-09-analyzing-%20proportions/)) |
| hypothesized value             |                                   |
|                                | $\chi^2$ g oodness-of-fit test    |
|                                | with two categories (use if       |
|                                | sample size is too large for the  |
|                                | binomial test) ([Lecture 1        |
|                                | 0](.                              |
|                                | ./l%20ec%%202%2%20%2000t%2%200u%2 |
|                                | 0%20%20re-%20%2%20010-fi%20%20%20 |
|                                | t%%%2020%20%2020t%20i%20%20n%20g% |
|                                | 20-%20%20pro%20%20bab%20%20%%%202 |
|                                | 0%2%20020ili%%2020ty%20%%2020-%20 |
|                                | mod%20e%20l%%2%2002%2%20%2000s-%2 |
|                                | 0t%%2020o-fre%2%200%20q%%2020u%2% |
|                                | 200e%20%%20%%202%20020ncy-data/)) |
+--------------------------------+-----------------------------------+
| Use frequency data to test the | $\chi^2$ g oodness-of-fit test    |
| fit of a specific population   | ([Lecture 1                       |
| model                          | 0](.                              |
|                                | ./l%20ec%%202%2%20%2000t%2%200u%2 |
|                                | 0%20%20re-%20%2%20010-fi%20%20%20 |
|                                | t%%%2020%20%2020t%20i%20%20n%20g% |
|                                | 20-%20%20pro%20%20bab%20%20%%%202 |
|                                | 0%2%20020ili%%2020ty%20%%2020-%20 |
|                                | mod%20e%20l%%2%2002%2%20%2000s-%2 |
|                                | 0t%%2020o-fre%2%200%20q%%2020u%2% |
|                                | 200e%20%%20%%202%20020ncy-data/)) |
+--------------------------------+-----------------------------------+

: Commonly used statistical tests for data on a single categorical
variable. These methods test whether a population parameter equals the
value proposed in the null hypothesis or whether a specific probability
model fits a frequency distribution. Adapted from Whitlock & Schluter
3e. W.H. Freeman and Company.

## Statistical tests for data on a [single numerical variable]{.keyword}

+------------------------------------+--------------------------------+
| Goal                               | Test                           |
+====================================+================================+
| Test whether the mean equals a     | One-sample $t$-test ([Lecture  |
| null hypothesized value when data  | 14](.%20./%20%%20%2020lect%20  |
| are approximately normal (possibly | u%20r%%20%202%2%20%2000e-1%%20 |
| only after a transformation) (13)  | %20204-t-t%20%20e%%20%2020s%20 |
|                                    | t%20%%20%%20%202020s-and-ci/)) |
+------------------------------------+--------------------------------+
| Test whether the median equals a   | Sign test ([Lecture            |
| null hypothesized value when data  | 16](.%2                        |
| are not normal (even after         | %20%200./lec%20%20t%%%20%2020% |
| transformation)                    | 2020u%20r%20%20%20e-%201%20%20 |
|                                    | 6-h%20%20and%20%20%%20%2%20%20 |
|                                    | 020lin%20g-%20%%20%2020viol%20 |
|                                    | a%20t%%20%202%2%20%2000ion%%20 |
|                                    | %2020s-of-%20%20a%%20%2020s%20 |
|                                    | s%20%%20%%20%202020umptions/)) |
+------------------------------------+--------------------------------+
| Use frequency data to test the fit | $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test  |
| of a discrete probability          | ([Lecture                      |
| distribution                       | 11                             |
|                                    | ](.%20%20./l%20%20%%20%2%20%20 |
|                                    | 020ect%20ur%20%%20%2020e-11%20 |
|                                    | -%20p%%20%202%2%20%2000ois%%20 |
|                                    | %2020son-d%20%20i%%20%2020s%20 |
|                                    | t%20%%20%%20%202020ribution/)) |
+------------------------------------+--------------------------------+
| Use data to test the fit of the    | Shapiro-Wilk test ([Lecture    |
| normal distribution                | 16](.%2                        |
|                                    | %20%200./lec%20%20t%%%20%2020% |
|                                    | 2020u%20r%20%20%20e-%201%20%20 |
|                                    | 6-h%20%20and%20%20%%20%2%20%20 |
|                                    | 020lin%20g-%20%%20%2020viol%20 |
|                                    | a%20t%%20%202%2%20%2000ion%%20 |
|                                    | %2020s-of-%20%20a%%20%2020s%20 |
|                                    | s%20%%20%%20%202020umptions/)) |
+------------------------------------+--------------------------------+

: Commonly used statistical tests for data on a single numerical
variable. These methods test whether a population parameter equals the
value proposed in the null hypothesis or whether a specific probability
model fits a frequency distribution. Adapted from Whitlock & Schluter
3e. W.H. Freeman and Company.

## Tests of association between [two variables]{.keyword}

+---------+-----------+----------------------+--------------------------------+
|         |           | **Type of            |                                |
|         |           | Explanatory          |                                |
|         |           | Variable**           |                                |
+---------+-----------+----------------------+--------------------------------+
|         |           | **Categorical**      | **Numerical**                  |
+---------+-----------+----------------------+--------------------------------+
| **Type  | **Cate    | Contingency analysis | Logistic Regression (Lecture   |
| of      | gorical** | ([Lecture            | 23)                            |
| R       |           | 1                    |                                |
| esponse |           | 2](../lecture-12-con |                                |
| Var     |           | tingency-analysis/)) |                                |
| iable** |           |                      |                                |
+---------+-----------+----------------------+--------------------------------+
|         | **Nu      | See next slide       | Linear C orrelation (Lecture   |
|         | merical** |                      | 21) and Spearman's rank c      |
|         |           |                      | orrelation (when data are not  |
|         |           |                      | bivariate normal) (Lecture 21) |
|         |           |                      |                                |
|         |           |                      | Linear regression (Lecture 22) |
|         |           |                      | and nonlinear regression       |
|         |           |                      | (Lecture 23)                   |
+---------+-----------+----------------------+--------------------------------+

: Commonly used tests of association between two variables. Adapted from
Whitlock & Schluter 3e. W.H. Freeman and Company.

## Comparing [group means]{.keyword}

+----------------+----------------+----------------+
| Number of      | Tests assuming | Tests not      |
| treatments     | normal         | assuming       |
|                | distribution   | normal         |
|                |                | distributions  |
+================+================+================+
| Two treatments | Pooled         | Mann-Whitney   |
| (independent   | variance       | $U$-test       |
| samples)       | two-sample     | ([Lecture 1    |
|                | $t$-test       | 6](            |
|                | ([Lecture      | .%2%20%200./%2 |
|                | 15](.%20./%2   | 0l%20%20ect%20 |
|                | 0lec%20%20ture | %2%200u%20r%20 |
|                | -%%202015-co%2 | %20%2%%2020%20 |
|                | 0%20m%%2020p%2 | 0e-16-h%20%20a |
|                | 0a%20r%20i%20n | n%20d%20%20%20 |
|                | g-two-means/)) | %20%20%20ling% |
|                | (assumes equal | 20%2%200-%20v% |
|                | variances, not | 20io%20%%2020l |
|                | commonly used) | at%%202%20%2%2 |
|                |                | 000i%20%20ons- |
|                | Welch's        | o%%2020f-%20%2 |
|                | two-sample     | 0%20a%%2020s%2 |
|                | $t$-test       | 0s%20%%20%%202 |
|                | ([Lecture      | 020umptions/)) |
|                | 15](.%20./%2   |                |
|                | 0lec%20%20ture |                |
|                | -%%202015-co%2 |                |
|                | 0%20m%%2020p%2 |                |
|                | 0a%20r%20i%20n |                |
|                | g-two-means/)) |                |
|                | (when          |                |
|                | variances are  |                |
|                | unequal in the |                |
|                | two groups)    |                |
|                | (always use    |                |
|                | this one)      |                |
+----------------+----------------+----------------+
| Two treatments | Paired         | Sign test      |
| (paired        | $t$-test       | ([Lecture 1    |
| samples)       | ([Lecture      | 6](            |
|                | 15](.%20./%2   | .%2%20%200./%2 |
|                | 0lec%20%20ture | 0l%20%20ect%20 |
|                | -%%202015-co%2 | %2%200u%20r%20 |
|                | 0%20m%%2020p%2 | %20%2%%2020%20 |
|                | 0a%20r%20i%20n | 0e-16-h%20%20a |
|                | g-two-means/)) | n%20d%20%20%20 |
|                |                | %20%20%20ling% |
|                |                | 20%2%200-%20v% |
|                |                | 20io%20%%2020l |
|                |                | at%%202%20%2%2 |
|                |                | 000i%20%20ons- |
|                |                | o%%2020f-%20%2 |
|                |                | 0%20a%%2020s%2 |
|                |                | 0s%20%%20%%202 |
|                |                | 020umptions/)) |
+----------------+----------------+----------------+
| More than two  | ANOVA (Lecture | Kruskal-Wallis |
| treatments     | 20)            | test (Lecture  |
|                |                | 20)            |
+----------------+----------------+----------------+
|                |                |                |
+----------------+----------------+----------------+

: A comparison of methods to test differences between group means
according to whether the tests assume normal distributions. Adapted from
Whitlock & Schluter 3e. W.H. Freeman and Company.

## Further resources

-   This is what you need to know for this course, but it is not a
    comprehensive list of hypothesis tests

-   Some other resources:

    -   [What statistical test should I
        do?](https://statsandr.com/blog/what-statistical-test-should-i-do/)
        Stats and R

    -   [Quantitative Analysis
        Guide](https://guides.nyu.edu/quant/choose_test_1DV), NYU
        Libraries

    -   [Statistical method selection
        tool](https://statkat.com/statistical-technique-selection/tool-for-selecting-a-statistical-technique-step-one.php),
        Statkat
